ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for martabak; 'pancake food product' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class for a stuffed, deep-fried dough product. Beef-stuffed and deep-fried specifics are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible category. Martabak is a stuffed, deep-fried savoury pancake; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Indonesian preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for martabak or Indonesian stuffed pancake. SNOMED CT does not have a specific food concept for this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of 12 large pieces weighs approximately 400 g, providing around 1100 kcal, 50 g protein, 70 g fat, and 70 g carbohydrate per full portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg rolls, meat and shrimp, restaurant-prepared (FDC ID 173948) and Pancakes, plain, prepared from recipe (FDC ID 172052); adapted composite for beef-stuffed deep-fried martabak
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for martabak beef. Values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried meat-stuffed pancake/egg roll products and plain pancake, cross-validated against the input record's ai-generated values (confidence 0.7). The input values (energy 275 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 17.5 g per 100 g) are consistent with the USDA composite for a deep-fried, egg-and-beef-stuffed wheat-flour pancake and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain a specific martabak beef entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Pancakes, wheat flour, plain (GI ~67, analogous estimate applied to deep-fried wheat-flour martabak)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for martabak beef. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain wheat-flour pancakes (GI ~67, medium). Deep-frying and the protein/fat content of the beef-egg filling are expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain pancake; GI is conservatively set at 65 (medium). Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × net_carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100. For a 400 g serving: net carbohydrate = (17.5 - 1.5) g/100 g × 4 = 64 g. Therefore, GL = (65 × 64) / 100 = 41.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100 g; saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: At 450 mg sodium per 100 g (1800 mg per 400 g serving), a single full serving delivers 90% of the WHO 2000 mg daily sodium limit; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 pieces and request reduced martabak sauce and sambal to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour pastry (medium-high GI ~65); low dietary fibre 1.5 g/100 g; sweet soy-based sauce adds glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wheat-flour pastry has an estimated GI of 65 with only 1.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 41.6 per full 400 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to 2–3 pieces, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and avoid the sweet martabak sauce to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g (exceeds AHA 5 g/100 g threshold); trans fat 0.25 g/100 g; cholesterol 125 mg/100 g; sodium 450 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.25 g/100 g, both of which raise LDL cholesterol; combined with 125 mg cholesterol and 450 mg sodium per 100 g, this dish poses a high cardiovascular risk and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; beef provides arachidonic acid; saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds RA threshold; chilli in sambal is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish or limit to 1–2 pieces, omit sambal chilli, and choose anti-inflammatory side dishes to partially offset the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Martabak Beef (12 pieces) is a popular Indonesian savoury street food widely sold in Singapore. It consists of a thin, crispy wheat-flour pastry shell that is deep-fried until golden and filled with a seasoned mixture of minced beef, eggs, spring onion, garlic, and shallots. The dish is served in 12 large pieces alongside acar (Indonesian pickled vegetables in a sweet-sour vinegar brine), a sweet soy-based martabak telur dipping sauce, and sambal chilli. The exterior is characteristically crisp and oily from deep-frying, while the interior is dense, savoury, and egg-rich. Per 100 g, martabak beef provides approximately 275 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 17.5 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated), 17.5 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. The full 400 g serving (12 pieces) delivers approximately 1100 kcal and 1800 mg sodium, making it a high-calorie, high-sodium meal. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. Clinically, the refined wheat-flour pastry has an estimated glycaemic index of 65 (medium), but the low fibre content and sweet sauce produce a high glycaemic load per serving, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end products relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in martabak beef?
Is martabak beef high in sodium?
What is the difference between martabak manis and martabak asin?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for martabak beef exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried meat-stuffed pancake and egg roll products, cross-validated against the input ai-generated values (original confidence 0.7). The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain wheat-flour pancake as the reference food. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for deep-fried beef-stuffed martabak is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in any approved source consulted. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.25 g/100 g from the input record; this is plausible for a deep-fried product using partially hydrogenated oils and is retained. Glycaemic load per serving is calculated for the default 400 g (12-piece) serving: net carbohydrate = (17.5 - 1.5) g/100 g × 4 = 64 g; GL = (65 × 64) / 100 = 41.6. Two portions are listed in the input (400 g for 12 pieces, 200 g for 6 pieces); the 400 g portion is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The input listed two portions both marked is_default: true — only the 12-piece (400 g) portion is treated as the primary default. Country of origin is listed as Singapore in the input cultural field, but martabak is of Indonesian origin and is sold as a street food in Singapore; region_of_origin reflects this dual provenance. Iteration 3 fixes: (1) foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; (2) local_names populated for chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, and hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for this dish sold in Singapore; (3) aliases_by_language populated for chinese, malay, tamil, and hindi with entries that appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
